text: Rigid 1,3-disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) have emerged as essential linear bioisosteres for para-substituted benzene rings, often imparting superior pharmacokinetic properties to drug candidates. However, incorporating these motifs typically necessitates the use of [1.1.1]propellane—a volatile and unstable precursor—while existing alternatives often lack the broad reactivity of the parent hydrocarbon. In this work, we introduce stable thianthrenium-based BCP reagents designed for practical O-, N-, and C-alkylation. These reagents significantly expand the scope of bicyclopentylation, outperforming [1.1.1]propellane and other current benchmarks. By leveraging the unique redox and stereoelectronic characteristics of the thianthrene scaffold, we demonstrate the facile synthesis of these reagents via strain-release chemistry. Furthermore, the labile exocyclic C–S bond undergoes selective mesolytic cleavage upon single-electron reduction, generating BCP radicals that facilitate transition metal-mediated bond formations. The utility of this platform is showcased through the late-stage functionalization of complex molecules, maintaining high efficiency in the presence of diverse and sensitive functional groups.
